coredata.dc:title:
Evaluating antibiofilm efficacy of carbon dots against
Pseudomonas Aeruginosa
based on precursor molecule selection

coredata.dc:creator: Sawant, Vikram
Faleke, Hammed
Bhattarai, Bikash
Strickland, Sara
Pappas, Dimitri
Christopher, Gordon F.
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) represent a new class of potential antimicrobials with improved ability to penetrate biofilms and the ability to customize effects via selection of novel precursor molecules. However, the full range of potential antibiofilm impacts of CDs has not been well cataloged. In this work, the antibiofilm impact of CDs against Pseudomonas Aeruginosa biofilms are examined for a range of traditional metrics: planktonic antibacterial activity, cell viability within the biofilm, and biomass reduction. Additionally, the impact on biofilm viscoelasticity and dispersal are also characterized, which have not previously been reported. These new metrics are used to understand if CDs can be engineered to attack a biofilm's extra polymeric substance. To do this, CDs were made from 2 commonly used precursor molecules, albumin and citric acid. Additionally, one CD was made from cellulase, which in molecular form has enzymatic activity to break down polysaccharides found in the extra polymeric substance. All CDs exhibited some antibiofilm impact and weakened biofilms. However, the cellulase based CDs were most impactful particularly in reducing biomass, weakening biofilms, and causing dispersal. These results indicate that attacking biofilm EPS by using CDs that retain enzymatic activity is a promising avenue of potential treatment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biofilm
Carbon dots
Pseudomonas aeruginosa
Microrheology
Dispersion

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: SEM based bacterial packing density and biofilm porosity
Quantification of biofilm biomass using the crystal violet assay
Characterization of the cellulase-based, citric acid and albumin carbon dots
Impact of CDs on biofilm microstructure
Antibiofilm impact via biofilm antibacterial activity
Antibiofilm impact via reduction in EPS
Mechanisms causing antibiofilm activity of CDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to thank Dr. Kendra Rumbaugh and Hui Hua from TTUHSC for their assistance in providing agar plates and bacterial stock.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of citric acid, cellulase, and albumin carbon dot CDs. Size distribution of citric acid, cellulase, and carbon albumin dots as obtained from the TEM and Zeta potential citric acid, cellulase, and albumin CDs suspended in ultra-pure water.
FTIR, XRD, XPS of citric acid, cellulase, and albumin CDs.

The growth curves of Pseudomonas aeruginosa PAO1 obtained using OD600 at different concentrations of (a)citric acid-CDs, (b) albumin CDs, (c)cellulase-CDs and. (d) Bacterial viability was assessed after incubating the bacteria with the CDs for a duration of 15 h. The error bars represent the standard deviation, with the values presented as means ± standard deviations (at this scale not all error bars are visible).

Representative SEM images of Pseudomonas aeruginosa PAO1 WT biofilm treated with citric acid, albumin, and cellulase CDs Calculated Bacterial packing density and Biofilm porosity from all SEM images. Statistical Analysis were performed using ANOVA Dunnett’s test.

Confocal images of Pseudomonas Aeruginosa biofilms (PAO1 WT) 48-hour old biofilms were treated with CDs at concentration 5 mg/ml and then imaged. (a) Treated with Citric Acid, Albumin and Cellulase after staining SYTO 9 and PI. (b)Quantification of cell viability on Pseudomonas Aeruginosa biofilms. Image analysis was performed using Fiji (ImageJ, US National Institutes of Health, Bethesda, Maryland, USA).
Biomass quantification using crystal violet assays. The error bar represents standard error. Statistical Analysis were performed using unpaired student t test.

Quantification of dispersed bacteria from Pseudomonas Aeruginosa PAO1 WT biofilms treated with different CDs (citric acid CDs, cellulase CDs and albumin CDs) by varying concentrations (0.01–5mg/ml). The error bars represent the standard deviation, with the values presented as means ± standard deviations. Two-way ANOVA with Tukey-Kramer HSD test was used to compare the significance between CDs at varying concentrations and the control (*P < 0.05), as well as across different concentrations within each CDs group.

Impact of CD concentration α and creep compliance at a lag time of t = 1 s in PAO1 WT biofilms. The box is the middle 50 % of data, the line in the box is the median, the black plus sign is the mean, the vertical lines are the upper quartile and lower quartile. Stars above a line indicate a statistical comparison of means and below indicate a statistical comparison of variance. Stars directly above each concentration are comparison to PAO1 WT Control. Star values positioned between or above lines connecting 2 concentrations indicate comparisons between concentrations.
Percent increase of mean value of viscoelastic properties from untreated to treated for both PAO1 WT vs. PAO1 Δpsl. Treatment was 5mg/ml of Cellulase CDs, and values are compared after 10 h.
The number of tracked particles for each combination of strain, CD, and concentration.

ce:title.ce:bold: Evaluating antibiofilm efficacy of carbon dots against

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical Engineering, Texas Tech University, Lubbock, TX, United States

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Mechanical Engineering, Texas Tech University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Mechanical Engineering, Texas Tech University, Lubbock TX

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Texas Tech University, Lubbock, TX, United States

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Texas Tech University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Texas Tech University, Lubbock TX

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) represent a new class of potential antimicrobials with improved ability to penetrate biofilms and the ability to customize effects via selection of novel precursor molecules. However, the full range of potential antibiofilm impacts of CDs has not been well cataloged. In this work, the antibiofilm impact of CDs against Pseudomonas Aeruginosa biofilms are examined for a range of traditional metrics: planktonic antibacterial activity, cell viability within the biofilm, and biomass reduction. Additionally, the impact on biofilm viscoelasticity and dispersal are also characterized, which have not previously been reported. These new metrics are used to understand if CDs can be engineered to attack a biofilm's extra polymeric substance. To do this, CDs were made from 2 commonly used precursor molecules, albumin and citric acid. Additionally, one CD was made from cellulase, which in molecular form has enzymatic activity to break down polysaccharides found in the extra polymeric substance. All CDs exhibited some antibiofilm impact and weakened biofilms. However, the cellulase based CDs were most impactful particularly in reducing biomass, weakening biofilms, and causing dispersal. These results indicate that attacking biofilm EPS by using CDs that retain enzymatic activity is a promising avenue of potential treatment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: If published data will be made available through TTU public repository.

ce:section.ce:para: Biofilms are formed by sessile bacteria surrounded by a self-secreted extracellular polymeric substance (EPS), which provides mechanical strength and structure [1–3]. Within the biofilm, bacteria have favorable environments for growth, enhanced resistance to antimicrobial treatments, and increased strength against physical removal [4,5]. These factors increase both the survival and virulence of biofilm forming bacteria [6]. Pseudomonas Aeruginosa use biofilms to evade host immune system responses and treatment, causing serious infections in wounds. These infections can have severe negative outcomes in patients, costing millions of dollars annually [7,8].

There has been substantial effort in developing strategies to treat and remove biofilms, particularly those of P. aeruginosa. [9,10] Although there is not yet a standard of care [11], treatment typically includes multiple rounds [12] of physical debridement [13,14] followed by of topical antimicrobial treatment [14–17], which helps ensure drug penetration, aid biomass removal, and stop reinfection [18–20]. New treatment approaches have explored the induction of dispersal, which involves the transition of sessile bacteria to a planktonic state rendering them more susceptible to antimicrobials, via chemical triggers [21] or EPS breakdown [22–24]. Additionally, weakening of the biofilm mechanically to aid debridement via a range of mechanisms has been actively explored [25].

Another new treatment being explored is the use of Carbon Dots (CDs) to prevent or inhibit biofilm formation [26]. CDs are a class of spherical nanoparticles below 10 nm in diameter, which have shown great potential for a wide range of applications [27], including biomedical [28], due to their excellent physical, chemical, structural, and optical properties, which can be tubed via synthesis technique and/or precursor molecule selection. For biofilm treatment, CD’s have been identified as particularly interesting because their small size and composition allows penetration through the biofilm EPS, avoiding the typical trapping that occurs for traditional antimicrobials [29].

Mature biofilms have shown susceptibility to the generation of ROS by CDs composed of a range of precursors. Methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus biofilms were susceptible to ROS generated via laser induction of CDs with a unique hook-like structure that aided in bacteria attachment [30]. Mature biofilms of several species were treated/inhibited by ROS from N-doped CDs made of several precursor proteins [31,32]. Copper-doped CDs have effectively treated S. aureus biofilms through ROS without impacting surrounding healthy tissue [33]. Folic acid derived CDs treated biofilms of S. aureus due to ROS in acidic wound environments only [34,35].

Membrane disruption via electrostatic interactions has also been shown to be effective at inhibiting biofilms. Amine-based CD were able to inhibit biofilms formed from a range of bacteria species both gram positive and negative [36]. A number of CDs have been found to be adept at inhibiting the formation of P. aeruginosa through this mechanism: hydrothermal fabrication of CDs composed of citric acid and curcumin [37] and those composed from aminoguanidine [38] were found to damage bacteria cell walls of P. aeruginosa via electrostatic interactions and inhibit biofilm formation. Furthermore, membrane disruption can also occur via hydrophobic interactions [39,40].

The CD precursor properties can impact these effects, particularly the interactions between CDs and bacteria membranes. CDs composed from branched polyethyleneimine treated biofilms due to electrostatic interaction, which were more effective for higher MW precursors due to increased surface amine groups [41]. Similarly, the length of precursor alkyl chain was found to impact CDs ability to treat biofilms by impacting hydrophobic interactions with bacteria [42]. CDs can also retain properties of their precursor molecules, depending on synthesis technique due to exposure of functional groups on the surface. This has shown retention of both wettability [43] or antibacterial activity [44].

Although CDs have shown the ability to inhibit and treat biofilms, antibiofilm effects are generally attributed to antibacterial activity. This antibacterial activity is often evaluated in the planktonic state, which may not be indicative of biofilm antibacterial activity. In fact CDs with no antibacterial effect, have been shown to be antibiofilm and inhibit biofilm growth [45].

Some CDs have shown other antibiofilm effects. CDs synthesized from Echium italicum had minimum inhibitory concentrations (MIC) of 1 mg/ml against P. aeruginosa biofilms, which was attributed to their causing a reduction of expression in EPS related genes [46]. Iron doped citric acid based CDs with chitosan grafted to their surfaces were shown to cleave eDNA within mature biofilms in both S. aureus and P. aeruginosa [47].

CDs could potentially have several antibiofilm impacts such as biofilm antibacterial activity, dispersal, EPS disruption, and/or reduction in viscoelasticity. In this work, the impact of CDs on these 4 specific antibiofilm impacts are explicitly studied for a series of CDs that show a typical range of potential properties (charge, surface groups, and bulk morphology) on P. aeruginosa biofilms. Furthermore, one of the CDs studied is made from a glycoside hydrolase precursor, cellulase, which in its molecular form has been shown to cause dispersal[22, 23, 49] and reduce biofilm stiffness[50] in P. aeruginosa biofilms. We will also evaluate whether this CD retains its enzymatic activities while supplying additional antibacterial effects. To our knowledge, this is the first explicit study to look at the impact of CDs on dispersal and viscoelasticity

Citric acid CDs were solvothermally synthesized [48]. A homogenous mixture of 600 mg citric acid, 40 ml formamide, and 1.05 ml of ethylenediamine (Fischer Chemical) was transferred into a PTFE cup of the hydrothermal digester. Afterward, the sealed vessel was transferred into a 180 °C preheated oven and allowed to heat at constant temperature (180 °C) for 4 h. Then, the vessel was allowed to cool down to room temperature at which point the solution in the PTFE cup was filtered through a 0.2-micron pore nylon membrane disk filter. The carbon dots were precipitated from the filtrate by adding 100 ml of acetone after which the carbon dots precipitate was recovered by ultracentrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 10 mins while washing the carbon dots precipitate with ethanol: acetone (1:1, v/v) twice. The carbon dots were transferred into a glass petri dish and allowed to dry overnight. The carbon dots were kept in powder form at room temperature for further studies.

The albumin CDs were synthesized using the solvothermal method. 10 mg of albumin was dissolved in 40 ml of formamide (Fischer) and 1.05 ml ethylenediamine. The mixture was heated at 220 °C in the oven in a stainless-steel digestion vessel for 5 h. The product was filtered through a 0.2-micron pore nylon membrane disk filter, and the CDs were precipitated out with 100 % acetone. The albumin CDs precipitate was recovered by centrifuging at 10,000 rpm for 10 min and washed with a mixture of acetone and ethanol (v/v, 1:1). The albumin CDs was transferred into a glass petri dish and dried overnight. The dried carbon dots were stored at room temperature for further experiments. The albumin CDs were suspended in 50 ml of DI water.

Cellulase CDs were synthesized using microwave heating. A solution was prepared by dissolving 10 mg of fungal cellulase (source A. niger, 02150583; MP Biomedicals, LLC) in 40 ml of ultra-pure water and 1.01 ml ethylenediamine (Fischer). This solution was transferred into a Pyrex glass vessel and placed in the microwave reactor for 30 min at 300 W, 300 psi, and 220 °C. The solution was then filtered through 0.45-micron and 0.2-micron pore nylon membrane disk filters into a glass Petri dish, which was allowed to evaporate in a water bath at 60 °C to obtain the powdered form of the cellulase CDs. The CDs were kept in powder form at room temperature for further studies.

CDs size distribution was assessed using transmission electron microscope (TEM). To obtain the TEM images, an aliquot of CD solutions was dropped on a carbon grid and viewed using the Hitachi S-7650 TEM at 60kv. X-ray Diffraction (XRD) analysis was conducted on solid-state CDs samples using a Rigaku MiniFlex II to elucidate the carbon nature of the CDs.

Functional groups present on the surface of the CD was evaluated from the dried form of the CDs using ATR-FTIR spectrum obtained with a Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS10 Smart ITR FTIR with an ATR crystal attachment. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were obtained using a Physical Electronics PHI 5000 VersaProbe II Hybrid XPS.
CDs’ zeta potential was obtained using the Microtrac Stabino particle charge mapping system.

The antibacterial effect of CDs against P. aeruginosa PAO1 was characterized via optical density (OD600) measurements. Frozen bacterial stocks were stored at −20 °C. Prior to culturing, 10 ml of freshly prepared sterile LB liquid broth was added to a 100 ml Erlenmeyer flask. Using an inoculating loop, a small amount of frozen bacterial stock was added to the flask and cultured for 24 h at 37 °C, 200 rpm on a rotary shaker (Southwest Mini IncuShaker SH1000) to achieve full growth (OD600 = 1). Subsequently, they were subculture in fresh LB broth for 2.5 h, achieving a growth density of OD600 = 0.1 (5 *106 CFU/ml). Following this, the bacteria were diluted and subjected to re-incubation with various concentrations of CD solutions. Growth curves illustrating the change in OD600 over time were collected during a 24-h incubation period at 37 °C. Bacterial growth was monitored over 24 h, and a bacterial viability curve was calculated using the formula [49]:
(1)
Bacterial Viability
( % )
=
OD 600
( CDs )
OD 600
( C )
× 100
OD600(CDS)denotes the optical density value of bacterial solutions at various CD concentrations, and OD600(C) represents the optical density value of bacterial solutions without CDs.

Additionally, minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) values were determined using Agar dilution method. Planktonic bacteria were cultured in LB medium with increasing CDs concentrations. OD600 values and observations on agar plates were made after 12–16 h of incubation to determine the concentrations of CDs at which no bacterial growth was detected.

Two P. aeruginosa strains based on the widely used laboratory strain PAO1 were used in this work: Wildtype (referred to as PAO1) and strain lacking EPS component Psl (referred to as PAO1 ∆psl). Frozen bacterial stocks were stored at −20 °C. Prior to culturing, 10 ml of freshly prepared sterile LB liquid broth was added to a 100 ml Erlenmeyer flask. Using an inoculating loop, a small amount of frozen bacterial stock was added to the flask and cultured for 24 h at 37 °C, 200 rpm on a rotary shaker (Southwest Mini IncuShaker SH1000). A fresh and sterile LB broth in a test tube was mixed with overnight bacterial culture and incubated for 2.5 h at 37 °C, 200 rpm, after which the optical density at 600 nm (0.12) was measured using a spectrophotometer (Thermo Scientific GENESYS 20) to determine CFU, and the subculture was diluted in LB to achieve a final concentration of 5 * 106 CFU/ml.

The final concentration of 5 * 106 CFU/ml was inoculated into the microchannel Microfluidic channels were fabricated using standard soft lithography [50], using a design previously outlined [51]. Briefly, SU-8 molds (SU-8 2000, Microchem) were fabricated on silicon wafers. Channels made by pouring polydimethylsiloxane (Sylgard 184, Dow Corning) over the molds, which were bonded to PDMS-coated glass slides using air plasma. Microchannels were kept at 37 °C for 5 h to allow initial biofilm growth, microrheology probe particle solutions were injected.

After 48 h of biofilm maturation in microchannels, the biofilms were treated with CD solutions at various concentrations by completely replacing microfluidic channel volume. Untreated biofilms were not disturbed. Data was taken at 2 h after treatment for all concentrations except 5 mg/ml, which had data taken at 10 h.

To quantify colony forming units (CFUs), biofilms cultured in microchannels were removed via pipette and mixed with 1X PBS using a vortex mixer. Samples were then serial diluted and plated on agar. The agar plates were left at 37 °C overnight, and colonies were counted 12–16 h later. Experiments were repeated at least three times with at least three technical replicates.

After 10 h, the dispersed cells within the microchannel were extracted, while the residual biofilm underwent treatment with a phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) solution. The biofilm cells were then subjected to serial dilution and plating for quantification of colony-forming units (CFU) to determine CFU/ml. To assess the impact of cellulase, citric acid and albumin-based CDs, biofilm dispersion was quantified through Colony Forming Units per milliliter (CFU/ml) count. CFU/ml counts were assessed both before and after treatment with CDs and were normalized according to the microchannel size [52].

Particle Tracking Microrheology was used to probe biofilm viscoelasticity [53]. This technique tracks Brownian motion of probe particles embedded in a specimen. Each particle’s displacement reflects the material strain from the stress caused by particle movement. By looking at the mean square displacement of the particles as a function of lag time several relevant rheological properties can be obtained [54],

where, a = particle radius, kB = Boltzmann Constant, and T = Temperature, r is displacement, and t is lag time. The slope of the MSD vs lag time curve is a, which ranges from 0 to 1; α = 1 represents purely viscous diffusion, α = 0 is an elastic solid, and values between 1 and 0 are a measure of relative elasticity. Creep compliance, J, is a measure of inverse stiffness with larger values meaning less stiff materials. These values can be calculated for each individual particle or from the ensemble average across many particles.

Negatively charged, 1 μm carboxylate-modified polystyrene (Invitrogen, Cat# 13083) were used as probe particles. The particles were cleaned through centrifugation (4 cycles at 6000 rpm for 10 min) and suspension in clean deionized water. After cleaning, a particle solution with a concentration of 107 particles/ml was prepared via centrifugation in a mixture of 10 % glycerol and 90 % DI water [51].

Biofilms were grown in 4 different microchannels and particle tracking was conducted at 2 locations within a microchannel where separate biofilms were observed based on bacterial fluorescence; the channel design includes index locations to facilitate data collection. This results in 4 biological replicates and 2 technical replicates for each experiment, with the corresponding number of particle tracks in Table 1.

Distributions of values of a and J(t) are represented by box-whisker plots generated using Graphpad prism. Statistical variance analysis was conducted to examine the distribution of α and J levels in the comparison across various concentrations of different CDs groups found using the Fligner-Killeen test, a non-parametric method for comparing observed variance with expected distribution. Unpaired Mann Whitney(non-parametric) test was conducted to observe statistical differences in α and J mean ranks. The null hypothesis tested was for equivalence in the median ranks of distributions. Statistical significance is demarcated in figures by * for P ≤ 0.05, * * for P ≤ 0.01, * ** for P ≤ 0.001 and * ** * for P ≤ 0.0001.

Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) images were obtained using a Zeiss Crossbeam 540 scanning electron microscope. For SEM imaging of P. aeruginosa biofilms in PDMS microchannels, the process started by removing the bottom glass slide from the channels to expose the biofilms. The biofilms were then washed with 0.1 M sodium cacodylate (Electron Microscopy Sciences) for 10 min and fixed using an enhanced primary fixation method developed by Wells and colleagues [55]. Biofilms were fixed using a combination of alcian blue (0.15 %), glutaraldehyde (2 %), and paraformaldehyde (2 %) in 0.1 M sodium cacodylate buffer. After overnight fixation, the samples were washed and stained with 1 % osmium tetroxide and 1 % tannic acid. Following further washes, they were dehydrated through graded alcohols and HMDS, then air-dried. Finally, the samples were mounted on SEM stubs, coated with 12 nm of platinum/palladium, and prepared for imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: SEM based bacterial packing density and biofilm porosity
ce:section.ce:para: High-magnification SEM images (5000 ×) were obtained from biofilm structure for each sample. The packing density was quantified by manually counting the number of bacterial cells within a defined area(µm²) using ImageJ software. Counts were performed at least on 3 images. SEM images were further analyzed to assess biofilm porosity. Images were converted to binary format (black-and-white) using ImageJ thresholding. Porosity was calculated as the percentage of void (black) area relative to the total image area. Porosity (%)= (Void Area/Total Area) × 100.

Biofilm samples were imaged using a confocal laser scanning microscope (Olympus, FV3000). For staining, 2 dyes were used: Propidium Iodide for live bacteria (P1304MP, Invitrogen™ Thermo Fisher Scientific, ex/em 440/520) and SYTO 9 for dead (S-34854, Invitrogen™ Thermo Fisher Scientific, ex/em 517/617). 100 mg of Propidium Iodide (PI) and a 5 mM stock solution of SYTO 9 were prepared in DMSO. The biofilms were washed with water prior to being stained with 1 ml dye mixture containing SYTO 9 (3 µL), Propidium Iodide (3 µL), and water at a final concentration of 3.34 mM. After staining, the biofilms were incubated for 30 min and then washed once again with water to eliminate any redundant dye. The image was conducted using a 60X oil immersion lens.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantification of biofilm biomass using the crystal violet assay
ce:section.ce:para: The total biofilm biomass containing live cells, dead cells and extracellular polymeric substances (EPS) was quantified using the Crystal Violet (CV) assay by evaluating absorbance [56]. The biofilm was grown on a 96-well plate for 48 h, pursued by three washes with water to eliminate planktonic or unattached cells. The biofilm was then fixed with 98.7 % methanol and dried out in an incubator for 10 min. Afterwards, 100 µL of 1 % Crystal Violet (Gram CV) solution was added to each well, and the biofilm was stained for 15 min to allow the dye to attach to the attached biofilm. After staining, the wells were washed twice with water to remove excess dye. To solubilize the attached biofilm, 100 µL of 33.3 % acetic acid mixture was added to each well, and the plate was shaken for a period to confirm accurate solubilization. Biofilm biomass was quantified by computing the optical density (OD590 nm) utilizing a BIOTEK Synergy H1 Multi-Mode Microplate Reader. The experiment was performed three times with three replicates.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the cellulase-based, citric acid and albumin carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 depicts the size and charge of the synthesized CDs. The size of the citric acid CDs ranges from 2 nm to 6 nm with an average diameter of 3.9 nm, albumin CD’s size ranges from 2 nm to 72 nm with an average size of 7.8 nm, the cellulase CDs range from 3.0 nm to 7 nm with an average size of 4.2 nm. Unlike the other 2 systems, the Albumin appears to possibly have a bimodal distribution of size.

The zeta potential of particles informs the stability of the particles in solution [57] and their interaction with cell membranes via electrostatic interactions. Therefore, it is expected that zeta potential will impact both antibacterial activity of the CDs and ability to penetrate biofilms. The zeta potentials of the citric acid, albumin and cellulase CDs were −75.52 mV, + 4mV, and −53.67 mV respectively. The positive charge of the albumin CDs suggests that they might be less stable in solution, leading to a higher tendency to aggregate [58], thus potentially reducing their ability to penetrate biofilms effectively

XRD data ( Fig. 2) of all 3 CDs reveals significant difference between the citric acid and albumin in comparison to the cellulase. Citric acid and Albumin exhibit sharp peak signatures at ∼ 27° and ∼27.2° respectively. The cellulase CDs broad peak centered at ∼23.4° indicates these carbon dots are amorphous. CDs' amorphic nature also contributes to carbon dots' stability in solution due to the presence of heteroatoms [59].

The elemental composition and surface chemistry of the CDs was characterized using XPS (Fig. 2). The survey spectra of citric acid confirm the presence of carbon (59.7 %), nitrogen (25.5 %), and oxygen (13.7 %). For albumin presence of carbon (57.9 %), nitrogen (36.2 %), and oxygen (5.9 %) respectively, indicating a slightly smaller degree of carbonization in the albumin CDs. However, in the case of cellulase, the atomic ratios of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen could not be determined due to the presence of sodium. The sodium Auger peak (Na KLL) often appears around 535 eV in XPS analysis and is significantly broader/overlap than the typical oxygen peak.

ATR-FTIR was used to find the presence of functional groups on CD surfaces. The cellulase CD in comparison to the Citric acid and albumin show a more complicated and varied range of surface groups (Fig. 2). The citric acid CD has the least complex surface. The cellulase and albumin CDs appear significantly more complicated with cellulase appearing to have the most complicated surface structures. Overall, these results suggest the larger molecular weight of the cellulase resulted in a more amorphous bulk and more complicated surface in comparison to the smaller molecule citric acid and albumin CDs.

Fig. 3 displays results of CDs’ impact on planktonic PAO1 bacteria growth. Looking at the viability curves (Fig. 3a-c), all CDs tested had negligible impact on the bacteria growth at concentrations below 1 mg/ml.

All 3 CDs exhibited some inhibition of bacteria growth starting at 1mg/ml. Citric acid CDs stopped bacteria growth after 10 h, leaving bacteria concentrations at a steady plateau. Increasing the concentration continued to have improved impact; at 5 mg/ml the bacteria seemed to have been completely killed after 24 h of testing. Albumin CDs never stopped bacteria growth completely, and the bacteria population continued to grow at all concentrations. Increasing concentrations of albumin CDs resulted in slower growth. Cellulase CDs had significantly more impact. Like citric acid CDs, 1mg/ml stopped bacteria growth to a steady state value at 10 h. At 3mg/ml, the bacteria population did not grow from initial levels. Increasing concentration to 5mg/ml resulted in total inhibition of bacteria growth and complete eradication of bacteria at the 10-hour mark.

Fig. 3d shows antibacterial activity of CDs against Pseudomonas aeruginosa at the 12–16 h mark from dilution methods. The results were consistent with Fig. 3a-c. At 5 mg/ml cellulase CDs reach a MIC. No MIC values were found in the testing range for the other 2 CDs.

As observed in the literature, the effects of different types of carbon dots on planktonic bacteria are typically driven by electrostatic and hydrophobic interactions [26,39,45]. Typical results have seen that gram negative bacteria are most impacted by negative charged CDs. The results here are typical of those reports; both negatively charged citric acid and cellulase CDs exhibited stronger antibacterial activity than the positively charged albumin CD.

The cellulase CD was the most impactful. Given the known impacts of long hydrocarbon chains influencing hydrophobic interactions to breakup bacteria membranes in previous studies [42], the longer organic functional group chains on the surface of cellulase CDs as revealed in ATR-FTIR are likely the cause of increased antibacterial activity of the cellulase CDs over the other CDs. In general, the bactericidal impact of the CDs was consistent with previous reports on the impact of hydrophobic and electrostatic interactions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact of CDs on biofilm microstructure
ce:section.ce:para: SEM images of biofilms were taken after 10 h of treatment for all CDs at 5 mg/ml as well as the control sample ( Fig. 4). The fixation procedure enables the capture. The control biofilm structure is typical of expectations, we see rod like healthy bacteria covered in EPS. The EPS appears as both a film over/behind bacteria as well as long stringing connections between bacteria and hairy coating surrounding bacteria in the control image.

Examining the 3 CDs we notice first that the Citric acid treated biofilm appears to have similar levels of EPS as the Control system. Both the albumin and the cellulase treated biofilms have some EPS, but they appear to have less EPS in these images compared to both the control and the citric acid systems.

Additionally, we notice that all systems show evidence of dead (observed as misshapen) bacteria. However, this is most evident in the albumin system which appears to have a large proportion of dead bacteria. The Citric acid system and the cellulase appears to have fewer dead bacteria in comparison to the albumin system. We should note this observation was consistent at several spots where images were taken.

To more quantitively evaluate these images, bacterial packing density and biofilm porosity were analyzed using all SEM images (Figure S1). Treatment with different CDs showed no statistical significance on bacterial packing density compared to the control (Fig. 4). However, biofilm porosity was statistically slightly increased for the Cellulase system compared to the control. This indicates that Cellulase CDs did disrupt biofilm microstructure (Fig. 4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibiofilm impact via biofilm antibacterial activity
ce:section.ce:para: Given the qualitative observations of cell death in SEM figures and observations of planktonic antibacterial activity, the efficiency of CDs as antibacterials on bacteria within the biofilm was quantitatively examined using confocal microscopy and cell staining as described in the methods sections ( Fig. 5). The antibiofilm antibacterial analysis was calculating as the percentage of viable bacteria based on pixel areas taken from the live and dead images.

Despite variations in shape, charge, surface functionalization, and chemistry all tested CDs are effective to some degree as biofilm antibacterial agents. As can be seen from the analysis of biofilm antibacterial activity, albumin is the most effective treatment for cell death within the biofilms, followed by cellulase and citric acid. These results are consistent with qualitative observations from SEM imaging but not consistent with the planktonic antibacterial results, which saw both cellulase and citric acid more effective than the albumin CDs. In fact, at these concentrations, the cellulase CDs were able to reach MIC, which clearly do not within the biofilm itself.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibiofilm impact via reduction in EPS
ce:section.ce:para: We also noted qualitative differences in EPS content between experiments in SEM imaging. To quantitatively assess EPS expression 48-hour old biofilms were treated with CDs at concentration 5 mg/ml and then crystal violet method was used to quantify the biofilm biomass ( Fig. 6). It should be noted that this method examines total biomass of the biofilm. In comparison to the control system, only Cellulase showed a statistically significant reduction in overall biomass of the biofilm after treatment. This is consistent with observations of less EPS within the SEM imaging and increased porosity. Although Albumin had strong antibiofilm antibacterial capabilities and did reduce biomass qualitatively based on SEM, the quantitative difference with the control was not found to be statistically significant.

At lower concentration (0.01 mg/ml), the three varieties of CDs exhibited a limited effect on biofilm dispersion ( Fig. 7). Citric Acid and albumin CDs appear to cause observable dispersal at 1 mg/ml; this effect did not increase with increasing CD concentration.

Cellulase CDs had the most impact on increasing dispersal, and consistent trend with concentration of increasing dispersal. However, this impact was only statistically significant for the highest concentration of treatment. The efficacy of the cellulase CDs in comparison to the citric acid and albumin CDs appears consistent with planktonic antibacterial impact but not the biofilm antibacterial impact.

The impact of CDs on biofilm viscoelasticity has not been established, nor has any correlation between specific CD properties and biofilm viscoelasticity. Here, the impact of CDs on biofilm viscoelasticity is explicitly studied using microrheology.

At a lower concentration (0.01 mg/ml), CDs exhibited minimal impact on biofilm viscoelasticity when compared to control for all precursors ( Fig. 8). All CDs had statistically wider distribution for a, indicating some impact on the heterogeneity of the biofilms, and that the CDs were locally impacting biofilm mechanics. Cellulase and albumin CDs exhibited minor but statistically significant impact in increasing α based on mean value. However, statistically the albumin and cellulase were identical. There was almost no impact on compliance for all CDs except albumin. This minimal impact appears consistent with the minimal planktonic antibacterial activity of CDs observed in Fig. 3.

At moderate concentrations 0.1 mg/ml, CDs has small antibiofilm impacts. Citric acid has a minor viscosifying effect on the biofilms, seen via small increase in the mean and median values of α and no change in the compliance. Heterogeneity increased in comparison to control systems as well. Cellulase CDs had a statistically identical impact as the citric acid CDs on a but made the biofilms slightly more complaint. Albumin was much more impactful, making the biofilm more compliant and more viscous.

At 1 mg/ml, citric acid was slightly more effective, but showed similar results to the 0.1 mg/ml. Cellulase CDs impact was more significant at this concentration, making biofilms more viscous and weaker compared to control and approached the efficacy of Albumin CDs at this concentration.

At 5 mg/ml, all CDs were most impactful at making biofilms more viscous with large statistically significant increases in mean/median α and more heterogeneity. However, cellulase CDs increased relative viscosity and compliance much more effectively than citric acid and albumin CDs. This behavior corresponded with the significant biofilm antibacterial activity, dispersal efficacy, and reduction of overall biomass caused by the cellulase CDs in comparison to the other systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanisms causing antibiofilm activity of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: At the lowest concentrations, all CDs exhibited little to no antibiofilm impact, which corresponds well with planktonic antibacterial results in Fig. 3. However, concentrations above 0.1 mg/ml all CDs showed a range of varying antibiofilm impacts (antibacterial, biomass, dispersal, and viscoelasticity), which did not correspond to the observed planktonic antibacterial trends in Fig. 3. Interestingly, these antibiofilm impacts were tested 2 h after treatment, which was ∼10 h before significant planktonic antibacterial effects were observed. Both the impact at lower concentrations and earlier times indicate that the mechanisms for effective planktonic antibacterial and antibiofilm effects are not similar.

Despite being the 2nd most effective antibacterial treatment, the citric acid CDs were the least effective antibiofilm agent. In SEM images (Fig. 4), there were some signs of dead bacteria, but these CDs were the lease effective as biofilm antibacterial agents. Furthermore, the EPS microstructure resembled the untreated system and there was minimal reduction in biomass. Given that dispersal is correlated to localized cell death [60] and EPS breakup, it was not surprising that these CDs were not particularly effective at dispersal. Given the importance of EPS in controlling the biofilm viscoelasticity and dispersal has been previously found to be correlated to increased compliance and viscous nature [61], it is unsurprising that the citric acid CDs showed little impact on biofilm viscoelasticity. In comparison to the other 2 CD systems, the citric acid CDs had the highest negative charge and the simplest surface groups (based on FTIR). This combination of traits appears to make the citric acid CDs poor antibiofilm agents although still capable of reducing viscoelastic moduli.

Despite being ineffective as an antibacterial treatment based on Fig. 3, albumin CDs were quite effective as an antibiofilm treatment. Examination of SEM and cell viability showed that the albumin CDs were an excellent biofilm antibacterial agent. Quantitatively, there was no statistical reduction in EPS/ biomass for the biofilms treated with albumin CDs. As previously mentioned both localized cell death [60] and EPS reduction [61] can correlate to increased dispersal. However despite being an effective biofilm antibacterial, albumin CDs were statistically no better at dispersal than citric acid CDs. Although often correlated with increased dispersal, these CDs still showed a marked increase in compliance and viscosifying behavior before the other 2 CDs.

Albumin was the only positively charged CDs and the surface structures were slightly more complicated than citric acid CDs based on FTIR. Although it was less effective in planktonic antibacterial tests, it appears the positively charged CDs were better at navigating biofilm microenvironments geared to protect bacteria, allowing them to reach and kill more bacteria in biofilms, resulting in reduction in viscoelasticity as well. It is interesting that the antibiofilm effects of albumin CDs plateaued at higher concentrations. This may indicate that the initial penetration provided immediate effect, but the lack of other antibiofilm impacts reduced overall efficacy.

The cellulase was the best overall antibiofilm agent, exhibiting antibacterial, dispersal, biomass reduction, and weakening of biofilms. They were the second strongest biofilm antibacterial, and the best in reduction of biomass/EPS. They showed the only significant increase in dispersal. Unsurprisingly, this combination of effects caused significant weakening of the biofilm, and at the highest concentrations the greatest reduction in mechanical strength of the biofilm.

The cause for the cellulase CD being most effective as antibiofilm agents is initially unclear. They share the same charge with the citric acids CDs which were the worst CD in antibiofilm effects and seemed to have limited penetration of the biofilm in comparison to the albumin CDs. The amorphic nature of cellulase CDs, which is not present in the other 2 CDs, indicate the presence of reactive functional groups, which could serve as a redox center to trigger the generation of reactive oxygen species to disrupt the biofilm [59], or may indicate that the cellulase CDs have functional groups on their surface which retain their precursor properties.

As previously mentioned, cellulase has been shown to cause dispersal[22, 23, 49] and reduce viscoelasticity[50] due to breakdown of EPS, specifically alginate and PSL. The cellulase CDs exhibited similar behaviors. This is consistent with the previous observations of other CDs to retain precursor characteristics [43,44], and our own hypothesis that the Cellulase CDs might impact EPS due to molecular cellulase’s known impact.

To better evaluate this hypothesis, PAO1 Δpsl strains were treated with cellulase CDs ( Fig. 10). These strains have no PSL, and when treated with molecular cellulase, their viscoelastic properties were not impacted [61]. We compare the increase in a and compliance between the untreated and treated biofilms for both the WT strain and the Δpsl. If our hypothesis is correct, we would expect the increase in these values for the Δpsl treated biofilms to be smaller than the WT, which is observed.

The initial goal of this work was to examine the specific anti-biofilm impacts of CDs, and to examine if CDs offered antibiofilm effects beyond antibacterial impacts. The results indicate that planktonic antibacterial activity does not necessarily correlate to antibiofilm properties. However, all CDs tested showed some antibiofilm capabilities with varying degrees of efficacy. Interestingly, all CDs did exhibit reduction in biofilm viscoelasticity, which could be attributed to the combination of specific antibiofilm mechanisms each CD exhibited. To our knowledge this is the first demonstration of biofilm mechanical weakening by CDs, and shows that broadly speaking CDs of a range of properties can impact viscoelasticity.

However, the results also indicate that precursor selection is incredibly impactful to the efficacy of any such CDs as antibiofilm agents. Citric acid, a simple and negatively charged CD, was least impactful on all antibiofilm activities. Whereas the most effective was a precursor that exhibited existing antibiofilm impacts, cellulase, that were maintained when synthesized into a CD. Importantly, by synthesizing into a CD it also became biofilm and planktonic antibacterial, providing an increased benefit. Although these results are specific to the systems and bacteria studied here, they suggest that selection of precursors with existing antibiofilm impacts may be a effective means of attacking biofilms in general. The retention of precursor behavior is of course also likely impacted by both precursor molecules and also synthesis technique, and warrants further study.

Gordon F Christopher: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Dimitri Pappas: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Sara Strickland: Methodology. Bikash Bhattarai: Methodology, Investigation. Hammed Faleke: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Vikram Sawant: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank Dr. Kendra Rumbaugh and Hui Hua from TTUHSC for their assistance in providing agar plates and bacterial stock.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfb.2025.114811
